fields.label: Hours of natural light used per day

explanation:
**Environmental impact** is one of the biggest challenges of the 21st century. Measuring your personal impact—CO₂ emissions, water use, waste—and taking concrete action is both an individual and collective responsibility.

## How the Calculation Works

This calculator shows how much energy and money you save when you use natural light instead of artificial lighting for a given number of hours daily. It applies the formula with your input values and flags any entries outside typical ranges.

## Final Notes

This calculation is an estimate for reference only. For critical decisions—financial, medical, or legal—consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly for accuracy.

faq.q: Why does the number of hours matter for electricity savings?
faq.a: Each hour without using a lamp saves you watts × hours. The more hours you use natural light daily, the more you accumulate in monthly and yearly savings.

faq.q: How much can I save by switching to natural lighting?
faq.a: It depends on bulb type and local electricity rates. A 60W incandescent bulb used 8 hours daily costs far more than an LED (8–10W), but using natural light saves the most.

faq.q: What's the difference between LED and incandescent bulbs?
faq.a: LED bulbs use 8–10W, while incandescent use ~60W for the same brightness. Switching to LED saves energy, but using natural light saves even more.

faq.q: How do I maximize natural light in my home?
faq.a: Use large windows, skylights, and light-colored paint. Position work areas near windows. Mirrors can reflect light deeper into interior spaces.

faq.q: Does natural light provide heating in winter?
faq.a: Yes. Solar gain through windows provides passive heating in winter. In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing windows maximize this effect.
